assumptions: Assume the question relates to consolidated financial statements of a non-investment-entity group.

Assume the declared intragroup dividend has been recognized as an intragroup receivable/payable and creates foreign currency exposure because the relevant group entities have different functional currencies.
Assume the recognized dividend receivable/payable is treated as a monetary item under standard IFRS terminology.
Assume the exposure is not designated as part of a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation unless stated otherwise.

approaches.label: General prohibition on intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items must generally be with external parties. Intragroup transactions are normally excluded from hedge accounting in consolidation.
approaches.practical_implication: A booked intragroup dividend receivable is not eligible in consolidation unless a specific IFRS 9 exception applies.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says only exposures with parties external to the reporting entity can be hedged in consolidation, subject to stated exceptions.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of a recognized intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 provides an explicit consolidation exception for foreign currency risk on intragroup monetary items such as receivables/payables when FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The intragroup dividend has been recognized as a monetary receivable/payable
The group entities involved have different functional currencies
The foreign currency gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedge relationship is formally designated and documented
The hedge effectiveness requirements in IFRS 9 are met
approaches.practical_implication: A recognized intragroup dividend receivable may be hedge-accounted in consolidation only through the monetary-item foreign currency exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify as a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if the FX exposure is not fully eliminated.
Paragraphs 6.3.1 and 6.4.1 require an eligible recognized item plus formal designation, documentation and effectiveness assessment.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup dividend
approaches.reasoning: The forecast intragroup transaction exception applies only while the transaction is highly probable and only if its FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss. Once the dividend is booked as a receivable, this is no longer the relevant route.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not justify hedge accounting for an already recognized intragroup dividend receivable by relying on the forecast-transaction exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction to be hedged only if it is foreign-currency denominated and the FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraph B6.3.5 explains that forecast intragroup transactions qualify only when they affect consolidated profit or loss; many intragroup flows do not.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: Net investment hedge accounting is a separate model for hedging an amount of net assets of a foreign operation. IFRIC 16 says this guidance applies only to net investment hedges and not by analogy to other hedge types.
approaches.practical_implication: A standalone intragroup dividend receivable should not be analyzed as a net investment hedge unless the hedged item is actually the net investment.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.1 identifies a net investment in a foreign operation as a distinct type of eligible hedged item.
Paragraphs 7, 8, 11 and 12 limit the model to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and state it should not be applied by analogy to other hedge accounting situations.
